blocks.text: The 1921 edition of the Doctrine and Covenants remained the standard in the Church for sixty years but was ultimately replaced by a new edition in 1981. The new edition had its origins in the early 1970s, when the Church’s seminary and institute program and adult Sunday school curriculum began focusing more heavily on the scriptures. That focus made clear the need for a Latter-day Saint edition of the Bible, which was published in 1979.

Early discussions among those working on the Bible project also reflected on the need for new editions of the Book of Mormon, Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price. That need became even more apparent in 1976. On March 25 of that year, the Council of the First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve, meeting in the Salt Lake Temple, approved adding two revelations to the Pearl of Great Price.

The first was a vision of the celestial kingdom experienced by the Prophet Joseph Smith on January 21, 1836, and recorded in his journal. The second was a vision of the redemption of the dead given to President Joseph F. Smith on October 3, 1918.

At the general conference of the Church on April 3, 1976, President Spencer W. Kimball appointed his first counselor, President N. Eldon Tanner, to present the action of the First Presidency and the Twelve for a sustaining vote of the Church members. With that vote, the two revelations became a part of the Church’s canon, or standard works, of scripture.

In June 1979, the decision was made to move these two revelations from the Pearl of Great Price to the Doctrine and Covenants. They thus became the first revelations added to this book of scripture since Wilford Woodruff’s Manifesto was included in 1908.

In June 1978, a historic series of events resulted in yet another addition to the Doctrine and Covenants. “Each first Thursday of the month is a day for fasting and the bearing of testimony by the General Authorities of the Church,” explained Gordon B. Hinckley, who at the time of the meeting was a member of the Quorum of the Twelve. Assembling in an upper room of the Salt Lake Temple, the general authorities of the Church met on June 1. They heard testimonies from some of their number and partook of the sacred emblems of the sacrament.

After the Seventy and the Presiding Bishopric left the room, Church President Spencer W. Kimball spoke to members of the First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve, raising with them “the question of extending the blessings of the priesthood to blacks,” a question that been discussed many times by Church leaders over the years. President Kimball had spent long hours by himself in the temple, praying and pondering about the matter.
At the temple meeting on June 1, 1978, “we joined in prayer in the most sacred of circumstances,” remembered Elder Hinckley. “President Kimball himself was voice in that prayer.”

Elder Hinckley recalled: “The Spirit of God was there. And by the power of the Holy Ghost there came to that prophet an assurance that the thing for which he prayed was right, that the time had come, and that now the wondrous blessings of the priesthood should be extended to worthy men everywhere regardless of lineage. Every man in that circle, by the power of the Holy Ghost, knew the same thing.”

A week later, on June 8, the members of the First Presidency and the Twelve approved a letter to announce the revelation. The next day, they met with the other general authorities to inform them of the action and then released the letter “to the Church and to the world.”

On September 30, 1978, during the semiannual general conference of the Church, President N. Eldon Tanner read the June 8 letter to the congregation and said, “Recognizing Spencer W. Kimball as a prophet, seer, and revelator, and president of  The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, it is proposed that we as a constituent assembly accept this revelation as the word and will of the Lord.” The congregation voted unanimously to accept the revelation.

The committee of apostles responsible for the 1981 Doctrine and Covenants—Elders Thomas S. Monson, Boyd K. Packer, and Bruce R. McConkie of the Quorum of the Twelve—designated Wilford Woodruff’s 1890 Manifesto as “Official Declaration–1” in the new edition and added the June 1978 letter on priesthood as “Official Declaration–2.”

Besides the added revelations, the committee and those working under its direction made other changes to the Doctrine and Covenants. The changes included new section headings. In the 1921 edition of the Doctrine and Covenants, the headings embodied both historical background information and summaries of the section contents. In the 1981 edition, the headnotes—which run across the width of the page—provided historical background. Chapter summaries—one column wide—give readers brief synopses of section contents.

In the 1981 edition, new footnotes follow the model developed for the Latter-day Saint edition of the Bible published in 1979. The footnote numbers correspond to the associated verses. Multiple footnotes related to a single verse begin over again with the letter a and continue with as many letters of the alphabet as are necessary for that verse. For ease of reading, the notes are divided into three columns per page. The footnotes also include references to the new Topical Guide, a condensed storehouse of gospel knowledge that became an integral part of the 1979 Latter-day Saint edition of the Bible.

Other changes in the 1981 edition include running heads with verse numbers on each page, an index integrated with that of the Book of Mormon and Pearl of Great Price, new maps, and a revised “Explanatory Introduction.” The introduction explains: “It is evident that some errors have been perpetuated in past editions, particularly in the historical portions of the section headings. Consequently, this edition contains corrections of dates and place names and also a few other minor corrections when it seemed appropriate (such as discontinuing the unusual names beginning with Section 78). These changes have been made so as to bring the material into conformity with the historical documents.”

According to the introduction, the special features of the 1981 edition were all designed “to help readers to understand and rejoice in the message of the Lord as given in the Doctrine and Covenants.”

A few years after the new editions of the Doctrine and Covenants and other standard works appeared, Gordon B. Hinckley, by then a member of the First Presidency, spoke publicly about their value. “On behalf of the First Presidency of the Church,” he said, “I thank all who have had a part—and they are many—in the production of new editions of our standard works with their valuable study helps.” He reviewed many of the new tools and explained the critical importance of feasting on the word of God.

“Where there are questions, or where cross-referencing will help, the tools in the new editions will be invaluable,” he said. Then he promised, “Through reading the scriptures, we can gain the assurance of the Spirit that that which we read has come of God for the enlightenment, blessing, and joy of his children.”
